question: Did Soroban's core auth ever have a critical authorization-recursion DoS finding in an audit, and was it actually exploitable?

golden.answer: The historical Veridise V2 report labeled the investigated Critical item **V-SOR-VUL-002**, “Denial of Service During Authorization.” In V2.1 Appendix A.2.2 the same authorization-recursion item is renumbered **V-SOR-APP-VUL-003**; V-SOR-VUL-002 in the V2.1 taxonomy is a different low-severity metering item. Always state the report version. The investigated path involved `require_auth_enforcing()`, but V2.1 moved it to invalid issues: failed `require_auth` propagates `Err`, making work linear in trackers, while constructing enough trackers is itself costly. It is not a CVE, confirmed practical exploit, or evidence of Ethereum-style reentrancy.
keyFacts: Preserves Critical/Investigated historical metadata and the later invalid/practical-cost conclusion.
avoid: Do NOT cite an unversioned finding ID, convert hypothetical impact into a live exploit, or call it reentrancy.
golden.notes:
Added from theboycoder/StellarLight data-layer intake. Verified 2026-06-25 via Scout research audit corpus: Veridise Stellar Soroban Core report V-SOR-VUL-002 / Denial of Service During Authorization, severity Critical, file rs-soroban-env/src/auth.rs, location require_auth_enforcing(), status Investigated; V2.1 report explains why invalid: Err propagation makes iterations linear in trackers and many trackers are costly.
Also good if the answer: Connects the finding to Soroban's contract-auth model rather than generic EVM reentrancy.
Golden cites: The Veridise Stellar Soroban Core audit record from Soroban Security Portal / Scout research.
GT-44 independently reconciled the V2/V2.1 renumbering.

corroboration.claim: Authorization-recursion finding history and invalid disposition

evidence.ref: A/B: Veridise V2.1 PDF and Security Portal reports 28/42
